summary: Kenneth demonstrates a covered call on HIMS that's deep in-the-money (stock at $58.08 vs $49.50 strike). He addresses whether to buy back the contract to avoid assignment, concluding it's not worth the $880/contract cost ($2,600+ loss) and instead accepts assignment as part of the strategy's design, then plans to re-enter via cash-secured puts or re-buying stock.
takeaways: When stock gaps significantly above strike (HIMS $58.08 vs $49.50), buying back costs $880/contract—calculate total loss before deciding to close
Accept assignment when cost to buyback exceeds acceptable loss threshold; it's built into the covered call strategy
After assignment, rotate into cash-secured puts or re-establish position the following week rather than fighting the cap
Covered calls intentionally cap upside—acknowledge this risk upfront; only suitable if you can accept getting called away at strike price
Covered call wheel strategy requires minimal active management—Kenneth's stated preference despite occasional capped gains

strategy_candidates.change: Establish pre-assignment buyback threshold rule: only repurchase covered calls if cost ≤ 20-30% of premium collected; otherwise accept assignment and redeploy capital into CSP or new CC cycle
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth paid $880 to buyback (losing $2,600+) when premium collected was only $535—video demonstrates mathematical illogic of fighting deep ITM calls; Brian's $1,250/mo target benefits from strict capital redeployment rules rather than emotional buybacks

title: Should you buy back your covered call if the stock skyrockets?! #shorts
